Why Evaluation Metrics Matter
Different problems require different metrics.
Accuracy alone can be misleading.
Metrics must reflect real-world cost of errors.
A model can look good numerically but fail in practice.

Limitation of Accuracy
Create a model to identify terrorists boarding flights with > 99% accuracy
Simple model: predict every single person as not a terrorist
> 800 million passengers on US flights every year
19 confirmed terrorists who boarded US flights 2000-2017
Accuracy = (800,000,000 − 19) / 800,000,000 = 99.9999976%
High accuracy does NOT mean good model.
Accuracy does not perform well with imbalanced data sets.

Discussion 1:
When Is Accuracy Misleading?
In which of the following scenarios would a
high accuracy
value suggest that the ML model is doing a good job?
A deadly, but curable, medical condition afflicts .01% of the population. An ML model uses symptoms as features and predicts this affliction with an accuracy of 99.99%.
An expensive robotic chicken crosses a very busy road a thousand times per day. An ML model evaluates traffic patterns and predicts when this chicken can safely cross the street with an accuracy of 99.99%.
In the game of roulette, a ball is dropped on a spinning wheel and eventually lands in one of 38 slots. Using visual features (the spin of the ball, the position of the wheel when the ball was dropped, the height of the ball over the wheel), an ML model can predict the slot that the ball will land in with an accuracy of 4%.
15

possible_slide_title:
Discussion 3: Precision vs Sensitivity,
Which One Matters?
visible_text:
Discussion 3:
Precision vs Sensitivity,
Which One Matters?
Consider two models, A and B, that each evaluate the same dataset. Which one of the following statements is true?
If Model A has better precision than model B, then model A is better.
If model A has better sensitivity (recall) than model B, then model A is better.
If Model A has higher precision and higher recall than Model B on the same dataset, then Model A is strictly better with respect to those two metrics.
18

Intrusion Follow-up
False negative = catastrophic
Recall protects security.
But Can We Ignore Precision?
Example: 5 intrusion cases and 95 normal cases
If we predict
all cases as intrusion
:
Recall = 5 / 5 = 100%
Precision = 5 / (5 + 95) = 5%
This creates constant false alarms.
Key Insight
High recall is essential for safety.
Precision cannot be ignored.
A practical system needs
very high recall with acceptable precision
.
20

possible_slide_title: Alexa: Most Important Metric is Precision
visible_text:
Alexa: Most Important Metric is Precision
Possible Errors
False Positive (FP):
Activated by background noise or normal conversation
False Negative (FN):
Fails to activate when user says “Alexa”
Which Error Is More Costly?
False positives
interrupt conversations and reduce user trust.
False negatives
only require the user to repeat the command.
Most Important Metric:
Precision
Among all activations, what proportion were truly correct?
Goal: Minimize false triggers to protect user experience and trust.

Classification: ROC curve
Area Under Curve (the ROC curve)
AUC measures how well the model ranks positives higher than negatives.
AUC = 0.5 → random
AUC < 0.5 → worse than random
AUC = 1.0 → perfect ranking
25
AUC evaluates ranking ability, not probability calibration.

AUC interpretation

possible_slide_title: Discussion 5: What Does AUC Really Measure?
visible_text:
Discussion 5:
What Does AUC Really Measure?
How would multiplying all of the predictions from a given model by 2.0 change the model's performance as measured by AUC?
(for example, if the model predicts 0.4, we multiply by 2.0 to get a prediction of 0.8)
It would make AUC better, because the prediction values are all farther apart.
No change. AUC only cares about relative prediction scores.
It would make AUC terrible, since the prediction values are now way off.
29

possible_slide_title: Discussion 5 solution: What Does AUC Really Measure?
visible_text:
Discussion 5 solution:
What Does AUC Really Measure?
How would multiplying all of the predictions from a given model by 2.0 change the model's performance as measured by AUC?
(for example, if the model predicts 0.4, we multiply by 2.0 to get a prediction of 0.8)
It would make AUC better, because the prediction values are all farther apart.
No change. AUC only cares about relative prediction scores.
It would make AUC terrible, since the prediction values are now way off.
AUC depends only on ranking, not absolute values.
If predictions are multiplied by 2: Relative ordering unchanged → ROC unchanged → AUC unchanged
30

Metric Selection Strategy
Balanced classes → Accuracy is reasonable.
Rare positive class → Recall, Precision, F1.
Ranking problem → ROC-AUC.
Asymmetric cost → Choose metric aligned with real-world risk.
